Several parts of the brain are key actors in the production of fear and anxiety. Using brain imaging technology and neurochemical techniques, scientists have discovered that the amygdala plays a significant role in most anxiety disorders. By learning more about how the brain creates fear and anxiety, scientists may be able to devise better treatments for these disorders. 

 The first psychotherapy treatment that was shown to be effective was ‘exposure therapy’, which essentially encourages people in a graded way to go into their feared situations and stay in them for as long as they can and build their confidence that way. Another is ‘group cognitive behaviour therapy’, a talking therapy that helps people to understand the link between negative thoughts and mood and how altering their behaviour can enable them to manage anxiety and feel in control. There are, of course, drugs that can help people with anxiety, but medication will not cure an anxiety disorder, it can only help to keep it under control while the person receives psychotherapy. 

 There is plenty of evidence that exercise can help with anxiety problems. When stress affects the brain, with its many nerve connections, the rest of the body feels the impact as well. Exercise and other physical activity produce endorphins, which are chemicals in the brain that act as natural painkillers. Additionally, getting physically tired can help people fall asleep faster and have deeper, more relaxing sleep. As many people suffering from anxiety often have problems with insomnia, just the ability to get a good night’s rest can change their perspectives. 

 Anxiety is a normal but highly subjective human emotion. While normal anxiety serves a beneficial and adaptive purpose, it can also become the cause of tremendous suffering for millions of people. It is important that people recognise excessive anxiety in themselves at the earliest, as treatment can be very successful. Leaving it untreated can be a misery.

question: In the passage, the information about anxiety treatments is organized in which of the following ways?
solution: Option 1 is correct as the passage reviews several treatment methods like exposure therapy, cognitive behaviour therapy, and medication, discussing their roles and effectiveness. Options 2, 3, and 4 are incorrect because they imply a focus on comparison, historical progression, or debate, which are not central themes in the treatment discussion.
option1: By discussing various treatment options and their effectiveness.
option2: By comparing different types of anxiety disorders.
option3: By outlining the historical development of anxiety treatments.
option4: By presenting contrasting viewpoints on the subject.
comprehension:
Anxiety is an emotion that all people experience from time to time, and we do that for good reasons. It has been built into us; we have inherited it from our evolutionary past, because, in general, anxiety has a survival function. It kicks in whenever we sense danger – we freeze, we devote all of our attention to the danger, and our bodies react with a big release of adrenalin, an increase in blood flow to the muscles, getting us ready to run as fast as we can or fight as fiercely as we might. 

 Anxiety also helps us to focus on things when we have deadlines and, if someone is driving too fast when we cross the road, it helps us to jump out of the way quickly. So, there is nothing wrong with anxiety in general, and in fact, we would have difficulties if we did not experience it to some extent. However, it can get problematic if the danger is imagined rather than real, or is something that is exaggerated. In those cases, particularly if the perceived danger is out of proportion to the real danger, and it is persistent and disabling, then there is a danger of an anxiety disorder. 

 Scientists are looking at what role genes play in the development of these disorders and are also investigating the effects of environmental factors such as pollution, physical and psychological stress, and diet. Several parts of the brain are key actors in the production of fear and anxiety. Using brain imaging technology and neurochemical techniques, scientists have discovered that the amygdala plays a significant role in most anxiety disorders. By learning more about how the brain creates fear and anxiety, scientists may be able to devise better treatments for these disorders. 

 The first psychotherapy treatment that was shown to be effective was ‘exposure therapy’, which essentially encourages people in a graded way to go into their feared situations and stay in them for as long as they can and build their confidence that way. Another is ‘group cognitive behaviour therapy’, a talking therapy that helps people to understand the link between negative thoughts and mood and how altering their behaviour can enable them to manage anxiety and feel in control. There are, of course, drugs that can help people with anxiety, but medication will not cure an anxiety disorder, it can only help to keep it under control while the person receives psychotherapy. 

 There is plenty of evidence that exercise can help with anxiety problems. When stress affects the brain, with its many nerve connections, the rest of the body feels the impact as well. Exercise and other physical activity produce endorphins, which are chemicals in the brain that act as natural painkillers. Additionally, getting physically tired can help people fall asleep faster and have deeper, more relaxing sleep. As many people suffering from anxiety often have problems with insomnia, just the ability to get a good night’s rest can change their perspectives. 

 Anxiety is a normal but highly subjective human emotion. While normal anxiety serves a beneficial and adaptive purpose, it can also become the cause of tremendous suffering for millions of people. It is important that people recognise excessive anxiety in themselves at the earliest, as treatment can be very successful. Leaving it untreated can be a misery.

question: While the bustling city streets were filled with the cacophony of honking horns and chatter of pedestrians, the serene countryside, where time seemed to move at a slower pace, <u>provide a welcome retreat for their seeking solace</u> amidst the chaos of urban living.
solution:
This question tests **verb tense consistency and pronoun usage in complex sentences**.

When you start a sentence in the past tense, you need to stick with it throughout—like keeping the same rhythm in a dance. The sentence begins with "were filled" and "seemed to move" (both past tense), so the main verb should follow suit. Also, "those seeking" is the proper way to refer to people doing an action, not "who seek" or "them seeking."

Option 1 → Wrong pronoun "who" and awkward "seek" instead of "seeking."

Option 2 → Present tense "provides" clashes with past tense context.

Option 3 → Present tense "provides" doesn't match the established past tense.

Option 4 → Perfect! Past tense "provided" and proper "those seeking" construction.
option1: provided a welcoming retreat for who seek solace
option2: provides a welcome retreat for them seeking solace
option3: provides a welcoming retreat for those seeking solace
option4: provided a welcome retreat for those seeking solace

question: The outbreak of COVID-19 resulted in a health crisis and a drop in economic activity that were without ______________ in history. While containing and ______________ the spread of the virus was the first priority of public authorities, reducing the ______________ of the disease and limiting the pressure on healthcare systems were also considered equally important.
solution: The context discusses an unprecedented event. 'Precedent' correctly indicates something previously unknown, fitting the historical context. 'Mitigating' suggests reducing effects, suitable for the first response to the crisis. 'Incidence' correctly refers to the rate or occurrence of new cases, fitting into the sentence smoothly.
option1: similarity; combating; prevalence
option2: precedent; mitigating; incidence
option3: equivalent; eradicating; toll
option4: parallel; controlling; outbreak

question: Most artificial intelligence (AI) applications in the fields of healthcare, industry, finance, transportation, and writing, rely heavily on deep learning and natural language processing. However, while AI systems can mimic existing styles and patterns, there is an ongoing debate about whether AI can truly possess creativity in the same sense as humans. ______________________________.
solution: The best choice connects the theme of creativity with AI's limitations. Option 3 fits perfectly as it addresses the debate over AI's creative capabilities by pointing out the qualities AI-generated works might lack, aligning directly with the concern about true creativity. Options 1 and 2 introduce new themes not directly connected to creativity, and Option 4 shifts focus to ethical concerns rather than creativity.
option1: Man has long feared the rise of the machine, his own creation becoming smarter and more intelligent than him
option2: High-performance computing has already proven a machine’s ability to perform advanced calculations far faster and more accurately than the human mind
option3: AI-generated works may lack the depth, emotional connection, and unique perspectives that come with human experiences and emotions
option4: AI usage raises a number of ethical concerns such as privacy issues, bias in algorithms, and the potential misuse of AI in many applications

question: One of the statements below contains a word used incorrectly or inappropriately. Choose the option which has the incorrect or inappropriate usage of the word.
solution:
The question is testing for word usage between **diffuse** and **defuse**. To **diffuse** means to spread out widely or scatter, like how perfume diffuses through a room or how light diffuses through frosted glass. Think of it as making something spread out in all directions, like dropping food coloring in water and watching it spread. To **defuse** means to remove the danger from a situation or literally remove the fuse from a bomb. When your mom defuses an argument between you and your sibling by distracting you with ice cream, she's taking the tension out of a potentially explosive situation.
Option 1 -> Correct usage. The aroma spreads/scatters throughout the house. Option 2 -> Correct usage. The stained glass scatters and spreads the light coming through. Option 3 -> Incorrect usage. The teacher should "defuse" (remove tension from) the argument, not "diffuse" (spread) it. Option 4 -> Correct usage. Using a fan to spread out and disperse the accumulated smoke.
Hence, Option 3 is correct
option1: Every morning, the aroma of freshly baked bread diffuses throughout the house, enticing everyone to the kitchen.
option2: The stained glass diffused the light filtering through the windows.
option3: Realising that the argument between the two groups of students was getting out of control, the teacher diffused the situation by quickly changing the topic.
option4: He used a fan to diffuse the smoke that had accumulated in the kitchen while cooking.

question: One of the statements below contains a word used incorrectly or inappropriately. Choose the option which has the incorrect or inappropriate usage of the word.
solution:
The question is testing for word usage between **bans** and **banns**. A **ban** is a formal prohibition or restriction that forbids something from happening. Think of it like a big "DO NOT ENTER" sign - when something is banned, it's completely off-limits. For example, if your school bans cell phones in classrooms, you better keep that phone tucked away or risk getting in trouble! **Banns** (with double 'n') are public announcements, traditionally made in churches, about upcoming marriages. These announcements give the community a chance to speak up if they know of any reason why the couple shouldn't get married. It's like giving everyone a heads-up and saying "speak now or forever hold your peace!"
Option 1 -> Correctly uses "bans" to mean prohibits - the act forbids child marriage. Option 2 -> Correctly uses "bans" to mean prohibits - the government forbids travel for those with passport issues. Option 3 -> Incorrectly uses "bans" when it should be "banns" - it's referring to public announcements that allow community objections. Option 4 -> Correctly uses "bans" to mean prohibits - organic farming forbids pesticide use.
Hence, Option 3 is correct.
option1: The Child Marriage Restraint Act bans the marriage of those who are below 18 years of age.
option2: The government bans anyone with irregularities in their passports from travelling abroad.
option3: Bans provide the community a chance to raise objections and ensure transparency.
option4: Organic farming bans the usage of all pesticides and involves manual weeding.

question:
The sentences given below, when properly sequenced, form a coherent paragraph. Each sentence is labelled with a number. Decide on the most logical order and enter the sequence of numbers in the space provided.
  1. The muddy concoction – made by grinding the kava plant’s roots and mixing it with water to create a dark, earthy liquid – holds a deep cultural significance throughout the region, and the drink is often shared between friends, guests, and colleagues.
  2. Regular kava drinkers often describe this blissful state as a sort of peace, a way for the body and mind to uncoil from itself.
  3. Called yaqona in Fiji, ‘ava in Samoa and sakau in Micronesia, kava has occupied a sacred role in Pacific countries for centuries.
  4. A swig (or two) taken from a bowl or shell – sometimes quite literally a coconut shell cut in half – can lead to profound relaxation, a sort of numbness that trickles down from your mouth through your shoulders and spine.
solution:
The passage talks about kava drink and its cultural significance in Pacific countries.

**Link A** (3 → 1): "The muddy concoction" in sentence 1 specifically refers back to "kava" introduced in sentence 3.

**Link B** (1 → 4): Sentence 4 describes drinking effects following the cultural description and preparation details from sentence 1.

**Link C** (4 → 2): "This blissful state" in sentence 2 uses pronoun reference to the "profound relaxation" described in sentence 4.

<hr>

**Introduction:** Sentence 3 is the introducing sentence because it first introduces kava and its regional names.

**Conclusion:** Sentence 2 is the concluding sentence because it provides the philosophical reflection on kava's effects.

<hr>

**Assembling:** The order flows from introduction to description to immediate effects to deeper reflection.

Hence the correct order is **3142**

question:
The sentences given below, when properly sequenced, form a coherent paragraph. Each sentence is labelled with a number. Decide on the most logical order and enter the sequence of numbers in the space provided.
  1. By contrast, in America, it is estimated that even after post-COVID decline, total giving came to almost USD 500 billion in 2022.
  2. There are no comparable data on different countries. But one report by Dasra, a Mumbai-based NGO, estimates total private giving in India came to about USD 13 billion in 2022.
  3. A strategic sort of giving, involving philanthropy professionals, a long-term lens, and big ambitions of transforming society is gradually emerging. It differs, of course, between countries.
  4. Researchers at the Chinese Academy of Social Sciences, put total giving in China at USD 21 billion in 2020.
  5. By any measure, however, organised philanthropy across Asia is on a much smaller scale than in the West.
solution:
The passage talks about emerging strategic philanthropy and comparing giving across different countries. Link A (3 → 2): "It differs, of course, between countries" in sentence 3 directly connects to "There are no comparable data on different countries" in sentence 2. Link B (2 → 4): Both sentences provide specific data on Asian countries (India and China), flowing naturally together as regional examples. Link C (4 → 1): "By contrast, in America" in sentence 1 contrasts directly with the Asian data provided in sentences 2 and 4. Link D (1 → 5): "By any measure, however" in sentence 5 provides a concluding assessment after comparing specific numbers from Asia and America.
Introduction: Sentence 3 is the introducing sentence because it introduces the concept of strategic philanthropy emerging globally. Conclusion: Sentence 5 is the concluding sentence because it provides the final comparative assessment about Asian versus Western philanthropy.
Assembling: Starting with the general concept, then providing Asian examples, contrasting with America, and concluding with overall comparison. Hence the correct order is 32415

question:
The sentences given below, when properly sequenced, form a coherent paragraph. Each sentence is labelled with a number. Decide on the most logical order and enter the sequence of numbers in the space provided.
  1. In this microgravity environment, your blood also tends to move towards the heart and head.
  2. Both effects are only short-lived and, after a brief period of readjustment on arriving back home, the heart and spine return to normal.
  3. The heart interprets this as an increase in the amount of blood in the body and that it needs to pump less, so it shrinks.
  4. In space with less gravitational force than on Earth, there’s less pressure on your spine and so it’ll get a bit longer, effectively making you as much as two inches taller.
solution:
The passage talks about physiological changes astronauts experience in space environment Link A (4 → 1): "In this microgravity environment" and "also" connect to space environment and add blood circulation effects Link B (1 → 3): "The heart interprets this" directly references blood moving to heart and head from sentence 1 Link C (3 → 2): "Both effects" refers back to spine lengthening and heart shrinking effects mentioned earlier
Introduction: Sentence 4 is the introducing sentence because it establishes the space/microgravity environment setting Conclusion: Sentence 2 is the concluding sentence because it summarizes both effects and their temporary nature
Assembling: Space environment introduction, blood circulation effect, heart response, then summary of both effects Hence the correct order is 4132

question: One of the statements below contains a word used incorrectly or inappropriately. Choose the option which has the incorrect or inappropriate usage of the word.
solution:
The question is testing for word usage between **callus** and **callous**. A **callus** is that tough, hardened patch of skin you get on your hands from gripping monkey bars too much or on your feet from wearing shoes that don't fit quite right. Think of it as your body's natural armor - it builds up to protect you from repeated friction or pressure, like a protective shield made of dead skin cells. **Callous**, on the other hand, describes someone who's emotionally hardened - like they've built up thick emotional skin that makes them insensitive to others' feelings. If your friend doesn't even blink when you tell them about your pet goldfish dying, you might say they're being callous about your loss.
Option 1 -> Correctly uses "callus" to refer to hardened skin causing discomfort. Option 2 -> Correctly uses "callus" to refer to hardened skin being examined by a doctor. Option 3 -> Correctly uses "callus" to refer to hardened skin protecting the foot. Option 4 -> Incorrectly uses "callus" when it should be "callous" - the phrase should be "callous indifference" meaning cruel, unfeeling indifference.
Hence, Option 4 is correct
option1: A build-up of the callus is one of the most common causes of discomfort.
option2: The doctor examined the callus but neglected to treat it.
option3: When it is not too thick, the callus protects the foot from damage.
option4: He received fifty lashes on his feet delivered with callus indifference.

comprehension:
Meta is recalibrating content on its social media platforms as the political tide has turned in Washington, with Mark Zuckerberg announcing last week that his company plans to fire its US fact-checkers. Fact-checking evolved in response to allegations of misinformation and is being watered down in response to accusations of censorship. Social media does not have solutions to either. Community review - introduced by Elon Musk at X and planned by Zuckerberg for Facebook and Instagram - is not a significant improvement over fact-checking. Having Washington lean on foreign governments over content moderation does not benefit free speech. Yet, that is the nature of the social media beast, designed to amplify bias. 

 Information and misinformation continue to jostle on social media at the mercy of user discretion. Social media now has enough control over all other forms of media to broaden its reach. It is the connective tissue for mass consumption of entertainment, and alternative platforms are reworking their engagement with social media. Technologies are shaping up to drive this advantage further through synthetic content targeted precisely at its intended audience. Meta's algorithm will now play up politics because it is the flavour of the season. 

 The Achilles' Heel of social media is informed choice which could turn against misinformation. Its move away from content moderation is driven by the need to be more inclusive, yet unfiltered content can push users away from social media towards legacy forms that have better moderation systems in place. Lawmakers across the world are unlikely to give social media a free run, even if Donald Trump is working on their case. Protections have already been put in place across jurisdictions over misinformation. These may be difficult to dismantle, even if the Republicans pull US-owned social media companies further to the right. 

 Media consumption is, in essence, evidence-based judgement that mediums must adapt to. Content moderation, not free speech, is the adaptation mechanism. Musk and Zuckerberg are not exempt.

|-----------|---------|
| 1 | Meta drops fact-checkers, amplifies bias |
| 2 | Social media dominates content consumption |
| 3 | Unmoderated content risks user exodus |
| 4 | Moderation needed, not unlimited freedom |

**From passage:** "unfiltered content can push users away from social media towards legacy forms that have better moderation systems in place."

**Thought Process:**

Question asks what's IMPLIED → Passage warns unfiltered content might push users away → Where would they go? To "legacy forms" (old media like TV, newspapers) → Why? Because they have "better moderation systems" → Option 4 (regulations) matches this.

🔴 **Option 2** → Misreads Passage → "Informed choice" is social media's weakness, meaning users CAN fact-check.

🟠 **Option 3** → Too Extreme → Passage warns users might leave for legacy media. "Never" is too strong.

🟢 **Option 4** → Correct → Directly matches: users may return to old media because of better moderation.
option1: uncensored content will always have more appeal than controlled content.
option2: social media's innate strength is the user's inability to fact check.
option3: social media can never be discarded by its users.
option4: older forms of media will regain users because of its controls.

option2: understand that algorithms cannot control its content.
option3: accept the current trends as emphasised by algorithms.
option4: readjust its interaction with competitors.

comprehension:
Meta is recalibrating content on its social media platforms as the political tide has turned in Washington, with Mark Zuckerberg announcing last week that his company plans to fire its US fact-checkers. Fact-checking evolved in response to allegations of misinformation and is being watered down in response to accusations of censorship. Social media does not have solutions to either. Community review - introduced by Elon Musk at X and planned by Zuckerberg for Facebook and Instagram - is not a significant improvement over fact-checking. Having Washington lean on foreign governments over content moderation does not benefit free speech. Yet, that is the nature of the social media beast, designed to amplify bias. 

 Information and misinformation continue to jostle on social media at the mercy of user discretion. Social media now has enough control over all other forms of media to broaden its reach. It is the connective tissue for mass consumption of entertainment, and alternative platforms are reworking their engagement with social media. Technologies are shaping up to drive this advantage further through synthetic content targeted precisely at its intended audience. Meta's algorithm will now play up politics because it is the flavour of the season. 

 The Achilles' Heel of social media is informed choice which could turn against misinformation. Its move away from content moderation is driven by the need to be more inclusive, yet unfiltered content can push users away from social media towards legacy forms that have better moderation systems in place. Lawmakers across the world are unlikely to give social media a free run, even if Donald Trump is working on their case. Protections have already been put in place across jurisdictions over misinformation. These may be difficult to dismantle, even if the Republicans pull US-owned social media companies further to the right. 

 Media consumption is, in essence, evidence-based judgement that mediums must adapt to. Content moderation, not free speech, is the adaptation mechanism. Musk and Zuckerberg are not exempt.
question: The inherent downside associated with social media is that it
solution:
**From passage:** "Yet, that is the nature of the social media beast, designed to amplify bias."

<hr>

**Thought Process:**

Question asks about the INHERENT downside of social media

→ Look for what's built into social media's design
→ Passage says social media is "designed to amplify bias"
→ Amplify = create and spread more of it; Bias = prejudice/unfair views
→ This is described as social media's fundamental "nature"
→ Option 3 matches this.

<hr>

🔴 Option 1 → Not Mentioned → Digital divide (access gap) isn't discussed as social media's inherent problem.

🔴 Option 2 → Not the Core Issue → Passage debates moderation vs. free speech, but doesn't say expanding freedom is the inherent downside.

🟢 Option 3 → Correct → "Designed to amplify bias" directly states social media creates and spreads prejudice as its inherent nature.

🔴 Option 4 → Opposite → Social media amplifies BIAS, which destroys objectivity, not reinforces it.
```
option1: does not address the problem of the digital divide.
option2: results in unremitting expansion of freedom of expression.
option3: creates and spreads much innate and acquired prejudice.
option4: reinforces existing objectivity among the users.

comprehension:
According to the French philosopher Jean Baudrillard, commodities available for consumption are not inherently negative things. Baudrillard tried to interpret consumption in modern societies by engaging with the 'cargo myth' prevalent among the indigenous Melanesian people living in the South Pacific. The Melanesians did not know what aeroplanes were. However, they saw that these winged entities descended from the air for white people and appeared to make them happy. They also noted that aeroplanes never descended for the Melanesian people. 

 The Melanesian natives noted that the white people had placed objects similar to the aeroplane on the ground. They concluded that these objects were attracting the aeroplanes in the air and bringing them to the ground. Through a magical process, the aeroplanes were bringing plenty to the white people and making them happy. The Melanesian people concluded that they would need to place objects that simulated the aeroplane on the ground and attract them from the air. Baudrillard believes that the cargo myth holds an important analogy for the ways in which consumers engage with objects of consumption. 

 According to Baudrillard, the modern consumer "sets in place a whole array of sham objects, of characteristic signs of happiness, and then waits for happiness to alight". For instance, modern consumers believe that they will get happiness if they buy the latest available version of a mobile phone or automobile. However, consumption does not usually lead to happiness. While consumers should ideally be blaming their heightened expectations for their lack of happiness, they blame the commodity instead. 

 They feel that they should have waited for the next version of a mobile phone or automobile before buying the one they did. The version they bought is somehow inferior and therefore cannot make them happy. Baudrillard argues that consumers have replaced 'real' happiness with 'signs' of happiness. This results in the endless deferment of the arrival of total happiness. 

 In Baudrillard's words, "in everyday practice, the blessings of consumption are not experienced as resulting from work or from a production process; they are experienced as a miracle". Modern consumers view consumption in the same magical way as the Melanesian people viewed the aeroplanes in the cargo myth. Television commercials also present objects of consumption as miracles. As a result, commodities appear to be distanced from the social processes which lead to their production. In effect, objects of consumption are divorced from the reality which produces them.
question: How can consumption be made more satisfying?
solution:
| Paragraph | Summary |
|-----------|---------|
| 1 | Baudrillard uses Melanesian cargo myth |
| 2 | Melanesians mimicked objects to attract planes |
| 3 | Consumers blame products, not expectations |
| 4 | Real happiness replaced with signs |
| 5 | Consumption seen as miracle, not production |

<hr>

**From passage:** "In everyday practice, the blessings of consumption are not experienced as resulting from work or from a production process; they are experienced as a miracle... commodities appear to be distanced from the social processes which lead to their production."

<hr>

**Thought Process:**

Question asks how to make shopping/buying things more satisfying

→ The passage shows the PROBLEM with modern shopping
→ People think products appear magically (like a miracle)
→ They don't think about how products are made (the work behind them)
→ This disconnect causes disappointment
→ The FIX would be understanding HOW things are made and the work involved
→ Option 2 says this.

<hr>

🔴 **Option 1** → Not the Answer → Passage mentions TV ads but doesn't say banning them is the solution.

🟢 **Option 2** → Correct → Problem = people don't connect products to the work that makes them. Solution = understanding this connection.

🔴 **Option 3** → This IS the Problem → Thinking products are "miraculous" is exactly what Baudrillard says is WRONG.

🔴 **Option 4** → Not Mentioned → Colonialism isn't discussed as part of the solution.
option1: By banning television commercials that promise real happiness.
option2: By understanding the connection between production and consumption.
option3: By recognising that commodities produce miraculous change.
option4: By rejecting colonialism and all other forms of economic oppression.

question: Which of the following is an argument made by Baudrillard?
solution:
**From passage:** "Baudrillard argues that consumers have replaced 'real' happiness with 'signs' of happiness."

<hr>

**Thought Process:**

Question asks what Baudrillard argues (what he believes)

→ Look for his main claim
→ He says people replaced REAL happiness with SIGNS of happiness
→ Signs = symbols/images of happiness (not actual happiness)
→ Example: having the latest phone is a SIGN you should be happy, not real happiness itself
→ So people care more about the SIGN than the REAL thing
→ Option 1 says this.

<hr>

🟢 **Option 1** → Correct → His main point: people value signs (symbols) more than real happiness.

🟠 **Option 2** → Too Narrow → TV ads are part of the problem, but not THE main cause he identifies.

🔴 **Option 3** → Wrong → Baudrillard says consumers THINK it's magical, not that it actually IS magical.

🔴 **Option 4** → Wrong Focus → He uses the myth to explain modern shopping, not to talk about Melanesian people's problems.
option1: Consumers value signs more than the real.
option2: Television commercials are at the heart of unhappiness experienced by consumers.
option3: Production and consumption are magical processes.
option4: Melanesian people coped with the inequality of colonialism by creating myths.

question: How does Baudrillard engage with the cargo myth?
solution:
**From passage:** "Baudrillard believes that the cargo myth holds an important analogy for the ways in which consumers engage with objects of consumption."

<hr>

**Thought Process:**

Question asks HOW Baudrillard uses the cargo myth story

→ He says the myth is "an important analogy"
→ Analogy = a comparison story to explain something else
→ He's comparing Melanesian people waiting for planes to modern people waiting for happiness from shopping
→ He's using this story to CRITIQUE (criticize/explain the problem with) modern shopping
→ Option 2 says this.

<hr>

🔴 **Option 1** → Wrong Direction → He uses it to show shopping's PROBLEMS, not that it's good (a blessing).

🟢 **Option 2** → Correct → He uses the story as a comparison (metaphor) to criticize modern shopping habits.

🔴 **Option 3** → Wrong Purpose → He's explaining consumer behavior, not talking about Indigenous people's suffering.

🔴 **Option 4** → Not Mentioned → Nothing about buying "more serious" things.
option1: He uses it to show that consumption is a blessing.
option2: He uses it as a metaphor to critique modern consumption.
option3: He uses it to describe the suffering of Indigenous people.
option4: He uses it to show that consumers should consume more serious objects.

question: Why are consumers unhappy with commodities that they have just bought?
solution:
**From passage:** "While consumers should ideally be blaming their heightened expectations for their lack of happiness, they blame the commodity instead."

**Thought Process:**

→ Passage directly explains this
→ People have "heightened expectations" = they expect too much

→ When it doesn't, they're disappointed
→ But they blame the product, not their unrealistic expectations

🔴 **Option 2** → Wrong → TV ads DO create hype. That's part of the problem, not a lack of hype.

option1: Because they have exaggerated expectations of commodities.

question: What is Baudrillard's position on total happiness?
solution:
Question asks what Baudrillard thinks about "total happiness" (complete happiness)

→ Find what he says about total happiness

→ "Endless deferment of the arrival of total happiness"
Endless = forever/never-ending;  Deferment = delay/putting off

→ So total happiness is always delayed, never arrives

→ It keeps getting pushed to the future (wait for next phone, next car, etc.)

→ Option 1 says this - perpetually (forever) delayed.

<hr>

🟢 **Option 1** → Correct → "Endless deferment" means it's always delayed, never comes.

🔴 **Option 2** → Wrong → Happiness doesn't come if you wait. It's delayed forever (never arrives).

🔴 **Option 3** → Not His Point → He doesn't say total happiness comes from focusing on production.

🔴 **Option 4** → Not Mentioned → Ethical shopping isn't discussed as leading to total happiness.
option1: It is perpetually delayed.
option2: It comes with patience and waiting.
option3: It prioritises production over consumption.
option4: It results from ethical consumption.

question: What is Baudrillard's position on consumption?
solution:
**From passage:** "They are experienced as a miracle... Modern consumers view consumption in the same magical way as the Melanesian people viewed the aeroplanes."

<hr>

**Thought Process:**

Question asks Baudrillard's overall view of consumption (shopping/buying things)

→ Look at how he describes modern shopping behavior
→ People think products appear "magically"
→ They replace real happiness with fake signs
→ They keep waiting for happiness that never comes
→ They act like Melanesians expecting magic from planes
→ This is NOT logical or rational behavior
→ Option 4 says this - irrational (not based on reason/logic).

<hr>

🔴 **Option 1** → Wrong → Utilitarian = practical/useful. But passage shows shopping is based on magical thinking, not practical thinking.

🔴 **Option 2** → Misreads Opening → He says products themselves aren't negative, but doesn't say the consumption PROCESS is positive.

🔴 **Option 3** → Not Discussed → Egalitarian = equal for everyone. This isn't talked about.

🟢 **Option 4** → Correct → Magical thinking, fake signs, endless waiting = irrational (illogical) behavior.
option1: It is a utilitarian process.
option2: It is a positive process.

option4: It is an irrational process.

question: Deepak is an unpleasant person, but we all ____________ because his sister is a close friend of ours.
solution:
The blank needs a phrasal verb meaning to tolerate or endure someone despite their unpleasant behavior. Option 1 -> put him down -> means to criticize or humiliate someone, which is the opposite of tolerance. Option 2 -> put him aside -> means to set something apart or ignore, but doesn't convey enduring someone's presence. Option 3 -> put up with him -> means to tolerate or endure someone despite their annoying qualities. Option 4 -> put along with him -> is not a standard phrasal verb construction and sounds grammatically incorrect.
When you have to deal with that one annoying coworker who chews loudly and tells terrible jokes, you just have to put up with them. It means tolerating someone's irritating behavior because you don't have much choice in the matter - like enduring a long car ride with a chatty relative during family vacations.
option1: put him down
option2: put him aside
option3: put up with him
option4: put along with him

question: We hope that the government's new policies will ______ a period of economic growth.
solution:
The blank needs a phrasal verb meaning to introduce or begin something new. **Option 1 -> usher in ->** means to introduce or mark the beginning of something new. Option 2 -> set in -> means to begin and seem likely to continue (usually negative), not appropriate for initiating policies. Option 3 -> turn up -> means to appear or arrive, not to introduce or begin something. Option 4 -> set forth -> means to state or present ideas clearly, not to initiate a period of growth.
When a new president takes office, their policies are expected to usher in a new era of prosperity. Just like an usher guides people into a theater, to "usher in" means to formally introduce or mark the beginning of something significant, opening the door to new possibilities.
option1: usher in
option2: set in
option3: turn up
option4: set forth

question: When she inherited some jewellery from a distant relative, she had no idea of its worth and decided ______.
solution:
The blank needs a phrase meaning to have something evaluated or assessed professionally for its value. Option 1 -> to get an approval -> means to receive permission or consent, not to determine value. Option 2 -> to get it appreciated -> means to have something valued or treasured, but not professionally assessed. Option 3 -> to have it appraised -> means to have something professionally evaluated to determine its worth or value. Option 4 -> to have it apprised -> means to be informed or made aware of something, not to assess value.
When you inherit mysterious jewelry from Great Aunt Gertrude and want to know if it's worth a fortune or just costume pieces, you need to have it appraised. This means getting a professional evaluation to determine the true monetary value of your treasures – because that "diamond" necklace might actually be worth more than your car, or it might be worth about as much as a candy necklace!
option1: to get an approval
option2: to get it appreciated
option3: to have it appraised
option4: to have it apprised

question: There are so many instances of one or more deer crossing the road, or just standing in the middle of the road, or else ____________; it is like the deer cannot hear the noise of the engines or see the headlights.
solution:
The blank needs a phrase describing deer movement that completes the pattern of road-related deer behaviors. Option 1 -> jumping under the road -> impossible and illogical; deer cannot jump underground. Option 2 -> foraging beneath the road -> nonsensical; deer don't forage underground beneath roads. Option 3 -> staggering with the road -> incorrect preposition and unclear meaning in this context. Option 4 -> bounding across the road -> describes natural deer movement that fits the context of road hazards.
When deer go bounding across the road, they leap energetically from one side to the other with their characteristic springy jumps. It's like watching nature's athletes in action, but unfortunately, this graceful movement often puts them directly in the path of oncoming traffic, creating dangerous situations for both deer and drivers.
option1: jumping under the road
option2: foraging beneath the road
option3: staggering with the road
option4: bounding across the road

question: Everyone wondered how the travel vlogger could go around the world all through the year and ______.
solution:
The blank needs an adjective describing a lifestyle of constant travel and movement. Option 1 -> itinerant life style -> correct; "itinerant" means traveling from place to place, perfectly describing a travel vlogger's lifestyle. Option 2 -> itinerary life style -> incorrect; "itinerary" is a noun meaning travel plan, not an adjective describing lifestyle. Option 3 -> iterative life style -> incorrect; "iterative" means repetitive or cyclical, not related to travel. Option 4 -> itinerary in his life style -> incorrect; grammatically awkward and "itinerary" doesn't describe the lifestyle itself.
When someone lives an itinerant lifestyle, they're constantly on the move, traveling from one place to another without a permanent home base. Think of a wandering musician or a travel blogger who never stays in one city for too long - they've embraced the freedom of an itinerant existence.
option1: manage his itinerant life style
option2: manage his itinerary life style
option3: manage his iterative life style
option4: manage his itinerary in his life style

question: Without a doubt, the widespread use of renewable energy is a key solution to climate change. However, it is not a ___________, as efforts in conservation are equally crucial.
solution:
The blank needs an idiom meaning a complete or perfect solution to a complex problem. Option 1 -> dead ringer -> means an exact duplicate or look-alike, not related to solutions. Option 2 -> silver lining -> refers to finding hope in difficult situations, not about complete solutions. Option 3 -> red herring -> means a misleading clue or distraction, unrelated to the context. Option 4 -> silver bullet -> means a simple, complete solution to a complex problem, fitting the context perfectly.
When politicians promise that one policy change will solve all economic problems, they're claiming to have found a silver bullet - a magical, all-encompassing solution that can fix everything with one shot. Just like the mythical silver bullet that could kill a werewolf, this idiom suggests a single, powerful remedy for complex issues.
option1: dead ringer
option2: silver lining
option3: red herring
option4: silver bullet

question: The labourers who were fired broke into the office building and destroyed some of the machinery. Rather than finding a solution to their problems, they __________
solution:
The blank needs a verb meaning to make a bad situation worse. Option 1 -> exacerbated -> means to make a problem or situation worse, which fits perfectly. Option 2 -> extended -> means to make something longer in time or space, not necessarily worse. Option 3 -> exaggerated -> means to overstate or make something seem bigger than it is, not to worsen it. Option 4 -> extenuated -> means to make something seem less serious by providing excuses, the opposite of what's needed.
When you exacerbate a situation, you're like adding fuel to a fire - you make an already bad situation even worse. If your friend is angry about something small and you start criticizing them too, you've just exacerbated the problem instead of helping to solve it.
option1: exacerbated the situation
option2: extended their troubles
option3: exaggerated their hardships
option4: extenuated the circumstance

question: Among scientists, the discovery of the double helix structure of DNA and the genetic code it incorporates is <u> widely regarded to be one of the most significant scientific discovery  </u> of the twentieth century.
solution:
This question tests idiomatic preposition usage and noun agreement.

The phrase "regarded as" is the standard idiom when expressing how something is viewed or considered. Think of it like saying "seen as" or "considered as" - you wouldn't say "seen for being" or "considered like," right? Additionally, after "one of the most significant," you need the plural form "discoveries" since you're selecting from a group.

Option 1 → "Regarded for being" isn't idiomatic; sounds like payment for services.

Option 2 → Perfect! "Regarded as" is correct, plus "discoveries" matches plural requirement.

Option 3 → "Regarded like" is wrong, and "discovery" should be plural.

Option 4 → Missing preposition makes it grammatically incomplete and awkward.
option1: regarded for being one of the most significant scientific discoveries
option2: regarded as one of the most significant scientific discoveries
option3: regarded like one of the most significant scientific discovery
option4: regarded being one of the most significant scientific discoveries

question: Thank goodness, the damage to the car was <u>neglectful</u>.
solution:
This question tests word choice and distinguishing between similar-sounding adjectives.

When describing damage as minor or insignificant, you want "negligible" - think of it as damage so small you can practically ignore it, like a tiny scratch on your phone case that you forget about immediately.

Option 1 → Correct! "Negligible" means insignificant damage - practically nothing to worry about.

Option 2 → Wrong form! "Neglecting" is a verb, not describing the damage.

Option 3 → Not a real word! "Neglectable" isn't standard English usage.

Option 4 → Wrong meaning! "Negligent" means careless, not describing damage severity.
option1: was negligible
option2: was neglecting
option3: was neglectable
option4: was negligent

question: Although the new policy aims to <u>increase efficiency, reducing costs, and enhancing employee satisfaction</u>, some employees feel that the changes are too abrupt and poorly communicated.
solution:
This question tests **parallel structure (parallelism)**.

Parallel structure means items in a series must use the same grammatical form, like three musketeers marching in step. When listing actions, keep verb forms consistent: "eat, sleep, repeat" not "eating, sleep, repeated."

Option 1 → Mixes verb forms: "increase," "reducing," "enhanced" — like mismatched socks.

Option 2 → "Reducing of costs" sounds awkward — you don't "reduce of" something.

Option 3 → Two base verbs, then a gerund — breaks the pattern halfway.

Option 4 → All three verbs in base form — perfectly parallel marching!
option1: increase efficiency, reducing the costs and enhanced employee satisfaction
option2: increasing efficiency, reducing of costs, and enhancing of employee satisfaction
option3: increase the efficiency, reduce the costs and enhancing employee satisfaction
option4: increase efficiency, reduce costs, and enhance employee satisfaction

question: <u>If the President knew that his allies would let him down</u> so suddenly, he would have handled them with the greatest care.
solution:
Solution: This question tests **third conditional sentences (unreal past conditionals)**.

The third conditional expresses hypothetical situations in the past that didn't actually happen. It follows the pattern: "If + past perfect, would have + past participle." Think of it like saying "If I had studied harder, I would have passed the test" when you actually didn't study hard and didn't pass. We can also invert it by dropping "if" and flipping the subject and "had."

Option 1 → "Had knowledge" is clunky—we need "had known" for proper grammar.

Option 2 → Perfect! Inverted third conditional: "Had known" = "If he had known."

Option 3 → "Could know beforehand" breaks the past perfect structure we need.

Option 4 → Mixes present "can" with past "knew"—like wearing flip-flops with snow boots.
option1: Had the President knowledge that his allies would let him down
option2: Had the President known that his allies would let him down
option3: If the President could know beforehand that his allies would let him down
option4: If the President knew that his allies can let him down

question: When I had to leave town due to office work, I <u>had my brother to give food to my dog</u> twice a day.
solution:
This question tests causative verb construction with "have."

When you "have someone do something," you're asking them to perform an action for you. The structure is: have + person + base form of verb (no "to"!). Think of it like giving a direct command through someone else. Example: "I had the mechanic fix my car" (not "to fix").

Option 1 → Correct! Uses proper causative structure: have + brother + feed.

Option 2 → Wrong verb form; "giving" doesn't fit the causative pattern.

Option 3 → Creates confusing relative clause; doesn't express the intended meaning.

Option 4 → Incorrect; causative "have" never takes infinitive "to feed."
option1: had my brother feed my dog
option2: had my brother giving food to my dog
option3: had my brother who fed my dog
option4: had my brother to feed my dog

question: A report published in Lancet Diabetes and Endocrinology has called for an overhaul of our understanding of obesity. An over-reliance on using Body Mass Index [BMI] as a metric has the peculiar effect of leading to both underdiagnosis and overdiagnosis of the condition. __________________________. BMI does not give accurate information about how fat is distributed in an individual’s body. It frequently fails to capture the true state of health of an individual. A person’s BMI may indicate they are “obese”, but their organs and bodily functions may be absolutely normal. Every individual is a unique constellation — not only of genes and other biological variables, but also socio-economic conditions and habits.
solution:
Left Link (Before → Option): BMI causes both underdiagnosis and overdiagnosis problems in identifying obesity conditions properly. Right Link (Option → After): BMI fails to show fat distribution and health accurately, with unique individual variations. We need to fit the blank where it explains why BMI causes diagnosis problems to maintain coherence. Option 4 accomplishes it best as "This is because BMI does not provide a reliable picture of health, nor any direct measure of fat."
Eliminating other options: Option 1: Contradicts the passage by claiming BMI can pinpoint obesity causes effectively. Option 2: Introduces irrelevant social media topic that doesn't connect to following BMI criticisms. Option 3: Contradicts the critical tone by suggesting BMI helps doctors prescribe treatments accurately.
option1: Obesity is the end result of multiple factors and BMI can pinpoint the cause of the problem
option2: Further, much of the information on diabetes, obesity or BMI available on social media is misleading
option3: BMI reading can help the doctor to accurately prescribe the appropriate dosage to reduce fat
option4: This is because BMI does not provide a reliable picture of health, nor any direct measure of fat

question: An island in Japan boasts of numerous dairy farms that own nearly one million cows, and supplies 70% of the milk sold in the country. These dairy farms have now begun to use cow manure to produce hydrogen. The methane from cow manure mingles with steam in a high temperature environment to produce hydrogen, which is used to electrify the local zoo. __________________________.
solution:
Left Link (Before → Option): The passage describes how Japanese dairy farms convert cow manure into hydrogen to power the local zoo. Right Link (Option → After): The sentence should provide a concluding evaluation or summary of this innovative energy process described. We need to fit the blank where it explains the significance of this cow manure to hydrogen conversion process to maintain coherence. Option 3 accomplishes it best as "This is an exemplary way of creating a sustainable source of energy using innovative technology" - it provides a natural evaluative conclusion that summarizes the sustainability and innovation aspects of the process just described.
Eliminating other options: Option 1: Abruptly shifts focus to India without smooth transition from the Japanese example. Option 2: Too abstract and doesn't capture the innovation/sustainability significance of the described process. Option 4: Makes unsupported broad generalization about Japan that goes beyond the specific example provided.
option1: The Indian government too, should replicate this, and use such technology to produce hydrogen
option2: It is a case study of a certain animal that is useful in providing energy for several other animals
option3: This is an exemplary way of creating a sustainable source of energy using innovative technology
option4: This shows how Japan has always used technology to help animals

question: As globalization held sway over the world, communities, which used to live in relative isolation, sought access to the wider world, and in the process, they parted with their own language and adopted a new lingua franca. The loss of language, however, does not merely mean the loss of a mode of communication or the loss of a few thousand words. __________________________. So, when a language dies, a way of thinking dies with it.
solution:
Left Link (Before → Option): The passage establishes that losing a language means losing much more than just communication tools. Right Link (Option → After): The sentence concludes that when languages die, entire ways of thinking disappear with them. We need to fit the blank where it explains what languages contain beyond mere communication to maintain coherence. Option 3 accomplishes it best as it states "Languages exist not only for the purposes of practical communication; they convey a linguistic community's entire mindset and its culture."
Eliminating other options: Option 1: Discusses revival movements but doesn't bridge what's lost beyond communication and thinking patterns. Option 2: Focuses on language vitality assessment rather than explaining what languages actually contain culturally. Option 4: Addresses language evolution over time but doesn't connect to mindset or cultural loss.
option1: A certain school of thought regrets the demise of local languages but in recent times revival movements have emerged across the world, especially in India
option2: A potentially endangered language can sometimes appear to be thriving, or on the other hand, it can show signs of declining
option3: Languages exist not only for the purposes of practical communication; they convey a linguistic community's entire mindset and its culture
option4: Since evolution and change in languages is a part of history, most of the languages spoken today would be scarcely recognizable from what they were a few thousand or maybe even a few hundred years ago

question: On the first day of January 2025, the Indian Meteorological Department [IMD] announced that 2024 was the hottest year on record. A study by the Council on Energy, Environment and Water shows that nearly eight out of ten Indians live in districts that are at risk of either a flood, a cyclone, or a drought. Nearly twenty three States in India are heatwave-prone. __________________________. In the summer of 2024, India recorded more than 44,000 cases of heatstroke and over 300 heat-related mortalities, as per the bulletin of the Ministry of Health and Family Welfare. Water reservoirs and the energy demand that keeps India powered are impacted too. During a ten-day long heatwave in Delhi, peak power demand was 16% higher than the previous year.
solution:
Left Link (Before → Option): The passage establishes India's vulnerability with statistics about climate disaster risks and heatwave-prone states. Right Link (Option → After): The passage provides specific 2024 data on heatstroke cases, deaths, and increased power demand impacts. We need to fit the blank where it explains how the established vulnerability translates into actual consequences to maintain coherence. Option 4 accomplishes it best as "The increasing heat stress remains a major challenge, affecting public health and economic productivity" directly bridges from general risk statistics to specific health and economic impacts documented in 2024.
Eliminating other options: Option 1: Contradicts the flow by mentioning unaffected population when focus shifts to actual impacts. Option 2: Introduces monsoon topic that doesn't connect to the health and power impacts discussed next. Option 3: "However" creates false contrast since compensation claim contradicts the documented losses and impacts shown.
option1: According to the Council, more than 20% of the population is not affected by climate change
option2: The record-breaking heat of the summer of 2024 resulted in an unpredictable and delayed monsoon
option3: However, the people of these districts are given sufficient compensation for loss of life and property
option4: The increasing heat stress remains a major challenge, affecting public health and economic productivity

question: Art can be ____________ because it encourages individuals to express their emotions through a creative outlet, allowing them to process complex feelings, reduce stress, and ____________ self-awareness.
solution: Coming soon!
option1: pleasing; decrease
option2: acceptable; disturb
option3: avoidable; mitigate
option4: therapeutic; enhance

question: Astronauts who stayed for an ____________ period of time at the International Space Station displayed a remarkable level of ____________ endurance and mental ____________.
solution:
It’s important to understand what each blank needs: > **Astronauts who stayed for an ______ period of time** > → An adjective meaning a *long duration*. > **displayed a remarkable level of ______ endurance** > → A word that naturally modifies *endurance*. > **and mental ______.** > → A noun meaning *psychological strength under stress*.
Eliminate incorrect options with the word fill-up that makes the whole option incorrect: | Option | Reason | | ---------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | | **1. extensive; dysfunctional; agility** | *Dysfunctional endurance* is contradictory, and *mental agility* refers to quick thinking, not sustained psychological strength. | | **2. explicit; stoic; integrity** | *Explicit period of time* is incorrect usage, and *mental integrity* does not convey endurance or coping ability. | | **3. expanded; stern; acuity** | *Expanded period of time* is unnatural, and *mental acuity* refers to sharpness, not resilience. |
Option 4 is correct as: **extended →** Correctly describes a long-duration stay. **physical →** Forms the natural collocation *physical endurance*. **resilience →** Accurately conveys psychological strength and the ability to cope under stress.
Final Answer: **extended; physical; resilience**
option1: extensive; dysfunctional; agility
option2: explicit; stoic; integrity
option3: expanded; stern; acuity
option4: extended; physical; resilience

question: While Curcumin, which is an ______ found in turmeric helps to reduce ______, extremely high doses of it can ______ headache and nausea.
solution:
It’s important to understand what each blank needs: > **While Curcumin, which is an ______ found in turmeric** > → A noun that correctly identifies *what curcumin is*. > **helps to reduce ______** > → A medical condition that curcumin is scientifically known to reduce. > **extremely high doses of it can ______ headache and nausea.** > → A verb meaning *to cause or bring about* negative effects.
Eliminate incorrect options with the word fill-up that makes the whole option incorrect: | Option | Reason | | --------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | | **1. enzyme; abrasion; infuse** | Curcumin is **not an enzyme**, and *abrasion* refers to surface damage, not a condition curcumin reduces. | | **2. alkali; infection; promote** | Curcumin is **not an alkali**, and *promote* contradicts the idea of causing adverse side effects. | | **3. alchemy; injury; cause** | *Alchemy* is irrelevant and unscientific in this context, making the option invalid outright. |
Option 4 is correct as: **ingredient →** Curcumin is a natural component found in turmeric. **inflammation →** Curcumin is widely known for its anti-inflammatory properties. **induce →** Accurately conveys causing side effects like headache and nausea at high doses.
Final Answer: **ingredient; inflammation; induce**
option1: enzyme; abrasion; infuse
option2: alkali; infection; promote
option3: alchemy; injury; cause
option4: ingredient; inflammation; induce

question: The notion of personhood is ____________ on something more than a particular type of genetic material within human beings: it arises only with the larger-scale structural ____________ of that material, which permits capacities like ____________, thought and moral agency.
option1: dependent; disorganisation; deconstruction
option2: interdependent; division; differentiation
option3: built; distribution; calibration
option4: premised; organisation; consciousness

question: Since chronic stress can ____________ the immune system, making individuals more susceptible to illness and ____________ their overall well-being, healthcare practitioners often recommend mindfulness practices and proper sleep to ____________ these negative effects.
option1: undermine; elevate; impede
option2: compromise; impair; counter
option3: paralyse; improve; diminish
option4: endanger; preserve; decrease

question: Psychologists urge users to remember that social media rarely reflects the full complexity of real life. Influencers often ____________ a carefully curated online persona, which can ____________ unrealistic standards and occasionally ____________ negative self-comparisons amongst their followers.
solution:
The sentence describes influencers presenting a curated persona that leads to unrealistic standards and causes followers to compare themselves negatively. We need words that match this logical flow.

Option 1 → endorse; foster; provoke
“Endorse a persona” is incorrect — you endorse a product or idea, not your own online identity.
Eliminate.

Option 2 → maintain; generate; trigger
“Maintain a carefully curated persona” fits perfectly, because influencers actively keep up a crafted image.
This persona can “generate unrealistic standards,” which makes sense as it creates them.
These standards can “trigger negative self-comparisons,” a natural psychological reaction.
This option is coherent and contextually accurate.

Option 3 → advocate; perpetuate; stimulate
“Advocate a persona” is unidiomatic — you advocate for a cause, not your own image.
“Stimulate negative self-comparisons” sounds unnatural in psychological context.
Eliminate.

Option 4 → profess; inspire; release
“Profess a persona” is awkward and incorrect — you profess beliefs, not identities.
“Release negative self-comparisons” makes no sense.
Eliminate.

Hence, Option 2 is correct.
option1: endorse; foster; provoke
option2: maintain; generate; trigger
option3: advocate; perpetuate; stimulate
option4: profess; inspire; release

comprehension:
CONVERSATION ANALYSIS: Read the following transcript and choose the answer that is closest to each of the questions that are based on the transcript. 

 Lucia Rahilly (Global Editorial Director, The McKinsey Podcast): Today we're talking about the next big arenas of competition, about the industries that will matter most in the global business landscape, which you describe as arenas of competition. What do we mean when we use this term? 

 Chris Bradley (Director, McKinsey Global Institute): If I go back and look at the top ten companies in 2005, they were in traditional industries such as oil and gas, retail, industrials, and pharmaceuticals. The average company was worth about \$250 billion. If I advance the clock forward to 2020, nine in ten of those companies have been replaced, and by companies that are eight times bigger than the old guards. 

 And this new batch of companies comes from these new arenas or competitive sectors. In fact, they're so different that we have a nickname for them. If you're a fan of Harry Potter, it's wizards versus muggles.  

 Arena industries are wizard-ish; we found that there's a set of industries that play by very different set of economic rules and get very different results, while the rest, the muggles (even though they run the world, finance the world, and energize the world), play by a more traditional set of economic rules. 

 Lucia Rahilly: Could we put a finer point on what is novel or different about the lens that you applied to determine what's a wizard and what's a muggle? 

 Chris Bradley: Wizards are defined by growth and dynamism. We looked at where value is flowing and the places where value is moving.  
  And where is the value flowing? What we see is that this set of wizards, which represent about ten percent of industries, hog 45 percent of the growth in market cap. But there's another dimension or axis too, which is dynamism. That is measured by a new metric we've come up with called the "shuffle rate." How much does the bottom move to the top? It turns out that in this set of wizard-ish industries, or arenas, the shuffle rate is much higher than it is in the traditional industry. 

 Lucia Rahilly: So, where are we seeing the most profit? 

 Chris Bradley: The economic profit, which is the profit you make minus the cost for the capital you employ is in the wizard industries. It's where R&D happens; they're two times more R&D intensive. They're big stars, the nebulae, where new business is born.
question: In the context of the conversation, "dynamism" most closely refers to
solution:
| Paragraph | Summary | |-----------|----------| | Para 1 | Lucia asks what "arenas of competition" means | | Para 2 | Chris explains how top companies changed from 2005 to 2020 - new companies 8x bigger replaced old ones | | Para 3 | Introduces "wizards vs muggles" - wizards play by different economic rules | | Para 4 | Lucia asks what makes wizards different | | Para 5 | Chris defines wizards: growth + dynamism. Dynamism = "shuffle rate" - how much bottom moves to top | | Para 6-7 | Wizard industries have more economic profit and R&D |
**From passage:** "But there's another dimension or axis too, which is dynamism. That is measured by a new metric we've come up with called the 'shuffle rate.' How much does the bottom move to the top?" Okay, so Chris is explaining what dynamism means -> he says it's measured by the "shuffle rate" -> this shuffle rate asks "how much does the bottom move to the top?" -> this means companies at the bottom are rising up fast -> positions are changing quickly -> that's what dynamic means!
Here's where students get confused: Options 1 and 4 talk about slow/stable things, but the passage is about rapid change. Option 3 is opposite (never-changing). Let's eliminate: 🔴 Option 1 -> opposite-meaning -> says "slow, gradual" but dynamism means fast changes (shuffle rate = bottom moving to top quickly) 🟢 Option 2 is correct -> captures the idea perfectly: "rapid and frequent changes in leadership and market position" = companies moving up and down fast = shuffle rate 🔴 Option 3 -> polar-opposite -> "never-changing" is the exact opposite of dynamism (which is all about change) 🔴 Option 4 -> opposite-meaning -> "stability and predictability" are what muggle industries have, not wizard industries (which have high dynamism)
option1: the slow, gradual growth and morphing of established companies.
option2: the rapid and frequent changes in leadership and market position within an industry.
option3: the never-changing reliance on established and unchanging business practices.
option4: the stability and predictability of traditional industries.

question: In the context of the conversation, the term "arenas of competition" refers to
solution:
**From passage:** "And this new batch of companies comes from these new arenas or competitive sectors." Lucia asks what "arenas of competition" means -> Chris explains by talking about industries (oil and gas, retail, pharmaceuticals, etc.) -> he then says "this new batch of companies comes from these new arenas or competitive sectors" -> so arenas = sectors = industries. Later, Chris also says "arena industries are wizard-ish" -> so he's calling whole groups of industries as arenas, not individual companies.
The trap here is Option 1 might tempt you because Chris does talk about companies, but read carefully - are arenas the companies themselves or the categories they belong to? 🔴 Option 1 -> scope-error -> too narrow! Arenas aren't specific companies, they're the industry categories those companies belong to (like tech, oil & gas, retail) 🔴 Option 2 -> irrelevant -> government regulations are never mentioned in the passage at all 🔴 Option 3 -> literal-misinterpretation -> takes "arenas" too literally as physical places; Chris means industry categories, not actual locations 🟢 Option 4 is correct -> "broad categories of industries" = sectors = what Chris calls "arenas or competitive sectors" in the passage
option1: specific companies that are considered to be powerful competitors.
option2: government regulations that control business competition.
option3: physical locations where businesses compete.
option4: broad categories of industries where companies engage in competitive activities.

question: "Muggles" refers to industries that
solution:
**From passage:** "Arena industries are wizard-ish; we found that there's a set of industries that play by very different set of economic rules and get very different results, while the rest, the muggles (even though they run the world, finance the world, and energize the world), play by a more traditional set of economic rules." This is a direct definition question! Chris says wizards play by different economic rules -> muggles play by traditional economic rules -> that's our answer right there!
The confusion happens because students remember wizards = good, so they think muggles = bad. But the passage says muggles "run the world, finance the world, and energize the world" - they're important, just different! 🔴 Option 1 -> wizard-characteristic -> "rapid and frequent changes" describes wizards (high shuffle rate), not muggles 🔴 Option 2 -> wizard-characteristic -> "high market cap growth" is what wizard industries have (45% of growth), not muggles 🟢 Option 3 is correct -> matches perfectly: "play by a more traditional set of economic rules" (from passage) = "operate under traditional economic principles" 🔴 Option 4 -> wizard-characteristic -> wizards are "two times more R&D intensive" = focused on innovation, not muggles
option1: are characterized by rapid and frequent changes.
option2: exhibit high levels of market capitalization growth.
option3: operate under traditional economic principles.
option4: are primarily focused on technological innovation.
